questions.question: What will be the unit digit of the cube of a number ending with 6?
questions.explanation:
First, the word "cube" means the product of a number three times with itself
So. let us find the cubes of the even numbers till ten.
{tex} 2^3=2 \times 2 \times 2=8 {/tex}
So, this does not match the condition.
{tex} 4^3=4 \times 4 \times 4=64 {/tex}
This too does not match the condition.
{tex} 6^3=6 \times 6 \times 6=216 {/tex}
Hence, this matches the condition.
So, the unit digit of a number whose cube ends with a 6 is also a 6.

Which of the following is Hardy-Ramanujan Number?
questions.explanation:
This story is very famous among mathematicians. 1729 is sometimes called the "Hardy-Ramanujan number".
There are two ways to say that 1729 is the sum of two cubes. {tex}1 \times 1 \times 1=1 ; 12 \times 12 \times 12=1728{/tex}. So {tex}1+1728=1729{/tex}
But also: {tex}9 \times 9 \times 9=729{/tex}; {tex}10 \times 10 \times 10=1000{/tex}. So {tex}729+1000=1729{/tex} There are other numbers that can be shown to be the sum of two cubes in more than one way, but 1729 is the smallest of them.
Ramanujan did not actually discover this fact. It was known in 1657 by a Frenchmathematician Bernard Franicle de Bessy.
But it got famous after the ramanujans above conversation.
So it's famously known as Ramanujan Number.

questions.explanation: Folding the sheet creates a right-angled triangle. Using the Pythagorean theorem, the hypotenuse is {tex}\sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = 10\text{ cm}{/tex}, which equals the original length.

questions.question: Which of the following is a property of a parallelogram?

options.option: The diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
The diagonals are perpendicular to each other.

questions.explanation: A parallelogram is defined by having opposite sides that are both parallel and equal in length, while its angles and diagonal properties can vary.

questions.question: Which of the following quadrilaterals has two pairs of adjacent sides equal and its diagonals intersect at {tex}90^{\circ}{/tex}?
questions.explanation: A kite is defined by having two pairs of equal adjacent sides, and its diagonals always intersect each other at {tex}90^\circ{/tex}.

questions.question: The angles of a quadrilateral {tex}A B C D{/tex} taken in an order are in the ratio {tex}3: 7: 6: 4{/tex}. Then, the {tex}A B C D{/tex} is a
questions.explanation: The angles sum to {tex}360^\circ{/tex} and calculate to {tex}54^\circ{/tex}, {tex}126^\circ{/tex}, {tex}108^\circ{/tex}, and {tex}72^\circ{/tex}. Two consecutive angles sum to {tex}180^\circ{/tex}, proving one pair of parallel sides.

questions.question: ABCD is a quadrilateral, in which {tex}AB=5 {~cm}, {CD}=8 {~cm}{/tex} and the sum of angles {tex}\angle {A}{/tex} and {tex}\angle {D}{/tex} is {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}. What is the name of this quadrilateral?
questions.explanation: Since {tex}\angle A + \angle D = 180^\circ{/tex}, sides {tex}AB{/tex} and {tex}CD{/tex} are parallel. A quadrilateral with one pair of parallel sides is a trapezium.

questions.question: The angle measurements of a quadrilateral are {tex}35^{\circ}, 49^{\circ}, 67^{\circ}{/tex}. The measure of fourth angle is
questions.explanation: The sum of all angles in a quadrilateral is {tex}360^\circ{/tex}. Subtracting the given angles from {tex}360^\circ{/tex} gives the fourth angle as {tex}209^\circ{/tex}.

questions.question: Let {tex}A B C D{/tex} be a rhombus. Then

options.option: Diagonals bisect the angles of {tex}A B C D{/tex}
The opposite angles of {tex}A B C D{/tex} are equal
The sum of the adjacent angles of {tex}A B C D{/tex} must be less than {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex}

questions.explanation: Options The opposite angles of {tex}A B C D{/tex} are equal and Diagonals bisect the angles of {tex}A B C D{/tex} are true for a rhombus, but option The sum of the adjacent angles of {tex}A B C D{/tex} must be less than {tex}180^{\circ}{/tex} is false because adjacent angles must sum to exactly {tex}180^\circ{/tex}.

questions.question: A parallelogram is a rectangle if

options.option: Each diagonal bisects the angles of the parallelogram

questions.explanation: A parallelogram becomes a rectangle if its diagonals are equal, each angle is {tex}90^\circ{/tex}, or its diagonals bisect its interior angles.

questions.question: A rectangle is a square if
questions.explanation: A rectangle already has equal diagonals and right angles. It becomes a square only when its diagonals intersect at a {tex}90^\circ{/tex} angle.

questions.question: Let {tex}A B C D{/tex} be a rectangle. Then
questions.explanation: In a rectangle, opposite sides are equal ({tex}AB=CD{/tex}), diagonals are equal ({tex}AC=BD{/tex}), and all interior angles equal {tex}90^\circ{/tex} ({tex}\angle ABC=90^\circ{/tex}).

questions.explanation:
For a number to be divisible by 9, the sum of its digits must be divisible by 9.

For 5x3:

{tex} 5+x+3=8+x{/tex}
To be divisible by 9:

{tex}8+x=9{/tex}
{tex}x=1{/tex}

questions.explanation: Any even digit

Last three digits = 536

The digital root of a number is 8. What is the digital root of the number plus 12?
questions.explanation: The digital root of 12 is {tex}1 + 2 = 3{/tex}. Adding this to the original digital root of 8 gives {tex}8 + 3 = 11{/tex}. Finding the digital root of 11 yields {tex}1 + 1 = 2{/tex}.

questions.question: Which of the following sums of digits will always be divisible by 3?
questions.explanation: A number is divisible by 3 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 3. The sum of the digits is {tex}3 + 6 + 9 = 18{/tex}. Since 18 is a multiple of 3 ({tex}3 \times 6 = 18{/tex}), any number with this sum of digits will always be divisible by 3.

questions.question: Find the smallest number divisible by 6 and 8 but not by 5.
questions.explanation: 24 is the smallest number divisible by both 6 and 8. The least common multiple (LCM) of 6 and 8 is 24, which means it is the smallest positive integer that both numbers can divide into without a remainder. Furthermore, 24 does not end in 0 or 5, meaning it is not divisible by 5, completely satisfying all conditions of the problem.

questions.question: A number leaves a remainder of 5 when divided by 7 and a remainder of 2 when divided by 9. Which of the following could be?
questions.explanation: Divide 47 by 7, which gives a quotient of 6 and a remainder of 5. Next, divide 47 by 9, which gives a quotient of 5 and a remainder of 2. Since 47 satisfies both given remainder conditions perfectly, it is the correct answer.

questions.question: If the product of two numbers is a multiple of 10 and one number is 5, what can be said about the other number?
questions.explanation: The product of two numbers is a multiple of 10, meaning it must end in 0. Since one number is 5, the other number must provide a factor of 2 to create a multiple of 10. Therefore, the other number must be an even number.

questions.question: The sum of five consecutive numbers is 95. What is the middle number?
questions.explanation: Let the five consecutive numbers be {tex}x-2{/tex}, {tex}x-1{/tex}, {tex}x{/tex}, {tex}x+1{/tex}, and {tex}x+2{/tex}, where {tex}x{/tex} represents the middle number. Summing these together gives {tex}(x-2) + (x-1) + x + (x+1) + (x+2) = 5x{/tex}. We are given that this sum equals 95, so {tex}5x = 95{/tex}. Dividing both sides by 5 yields {tex}x = 19{/tex}, which confirms that the middle number is 19.

questions.question: Which of the following numbers is divisible by 11?
questions.explanation: 4686 is divisible by 11. To check divisibility by 11, subtract the sum of digits in even positions from the sum of digits in odd positions. For 4686, the odd-position digits are 4 and 8, and the even-position digits are 6 and 6. The difference is {tex}(4 + 8) - (6 + 6) = 12 - 12 = 0{/tex}. Since 0 is divisible by 11, the number 4686 is divisible by 11.

questions.question: Find three consecutive numbers such that the first is a multiple of 7, the second is a multiple of 2, and the third is a multiple of 5. What is the smallest such set?
questions.explanation: The question asks for three consecutive numbers. Looking at the provided, 28, 22, and 30. These numbers are not consecutive because they do not follow one after another in counting order, meaning this specific choice is mathematically incorrect based on the problem's criteria.

questions.question: If 4x7y is divisible by 36, what are the possible pairs for x and y?
questions.explanation:
For a number to be divisible by 36, it must be divisible by both 4 and 9.

A number is divisible by 4 if its last two digits are divisible by 4. Checking the given options for the last two digits, {tex}7y{/tex}:

  • If {tex}y = 8{/tex}, 78 is not divisible by 4.

  • If {tex}y = 4{/tex}, 74 is not divisible by 4.

  • If {tex}y = 6{/tex}, 76 is divisible by 4.

  • If {tex}y = 9{/tex}, 79 is not divisible by 4.

Thus, {tex}y{/tex} must be 6, making the pair (1, 6) the only possible option. Testing the sum of digits for divisibility by 9 confirms this: {tex}4 + 1 + 7 + 6 = 18{/tex}, which is divisible by 9.

questions.question: If {tex}{A} \times {3}=1 {~A}{/tex}, then A is equal to
questions.explanation:
{tex}{A} \times 3=1 {~A}{/tex}, where 1 A is the two-digit number {tex}10+A{/tex}.
So,

{tex}3 A=10+A{/tex}
{tex}2 A=10{/tex}
{tex}A=5{/tex}

Check:

{tex} 5 \times 3=15{/tex}

which matches {tex}1 A=15{/tex}.

So, the correct answer is 5.

questions.question: If {tex}5 {~A} \times {A}=399{/tex}, then the value of A is
questions.explanation:
{tex}5 A{/tex} represents the two-digit number {tex}50+A{/tex}.
Given:

{tex} (50+A) \times A=399{/tex}

Check the options:

  • {tex}A=3: 53 \times 3=159{/tex}
  • {tex}A=6: 56 \times 6=336{/tex}
  • {tex}A=7: 57 \times 7=399{/tex}
  • {tex}A=9: 59 \times 9=531{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}{A}=7{/tex}.
So, the correct answer is 7.

questions.question: The digital root of 9876 is
questions.explanation:
The digital root is obtained by repeatedly adding the digits until a single digit remains.

{tex}9+8+7+6=30{/tex}
{tex}3+0=3{/tex}

Therefore, the digital root of 9876 is 3.
So, the correct answer is 3.

questions.question: The remainder when 579 is divided by 8 is
questions.explanation:
Divide 579 by 8:

{tex}8 \times 72=576{/tex}
{tex}579-576=3{/tex}

So, the remainder when 579 is divided by 8 is 3.
Therefore, the correct answer is 3.

questions.question: If two numbers are divisible by 9, their sum is always divisible by
questions.explanation:
Let the two numbers be {tex}9 a{/tex} and {tex}9 b{/tex}, where {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex} are integers.
Their sum is:

{tex} 9 a+9 b=9(a+b){/tex}

Since the sum is a multiple of 9, it is always divisible by 9.
So, the correct answer is 9.

questions.question: If a number is divisible by both 5 and 6 then it may be divisible by
questions.explanation:
If a number is divisible by both 5 and 6 , then it is divisible by their LCM.

{tex} \operatorname{LCM}(5,6)=30{/tex}

Therefore, such a number must be divisible by 30.
So, the correct answer is 30.

questions.question: A number is divisible by 90, if
questions.explanation:
Since

{tex} 90=2 \times 45=5 \times 18=9 \times 10{/tex}

a number divisible by both factors in any of these pairs will be divisible by 90.

  • Divisible by 2 and {tex}\Longrightarrow{/tex} 45 divisible by 90
  • Divisible by 5 and 18 {tex}\Longrightarrow{/tex} divisible by 90
  • Divisible by 9 and {tex}10 \Longrightarrow{/tex} divisible by 90

Therefore, all of the given conditions ensure divisibility by 90.

questions.question: Which of the following numbers are divisible by 6?
questions.explanation:
A number is divisible by 6 if it is divisible by both 2 and 3.

  • {tex}297143 \rightarrow{/tex} odd, so not divisible by 2.
  • {tex}1790184 \rightarrow{/tex} even, and {tex}1+7+9+0+1+8+4=30{/tex}, which is divisible by 3.
  • {tex}291245 \rightarrow{/tex} odd, so not divisible by 2.
  • {tex}1840791 \rightarrow{/tex} odd, so not divisible by 2.

Therefore, only 1790184 is divisible by 6.

questions.question: Which of the following numbers are divisible by 9?
questions.explanation:
A number is divisible by 9 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 9.

  • {tex}672 \rightarrow 6+7+2=15{/tex}, not divisible by 9
  • {tex}5652 \rightarrow 5+6+5+2=18{/tex}, divisible by 9
  • {tex}4847 \rightarrow 4+8+4+7=23{/tex}, not divisible by 9

So, 5652 is divisible by 9.

questions.question: The number 1265 is divisible by
questions.explanation:
Check divisibility by 11 using the rule:

{tex} (1+6)-(2+5)=7-7=0{/tex}
Since the difference is 0, the number is divisible by 11.
So, 1265 is divisible by 11.

questions.question: The numbers 116 and 365 are divisible by
questions.explanation:
Check divisibility of both numbers by 5 and 7.
A number divisible by 5 must end in 0 or 5.
116 does not end in 0 or 5.
365 is divisible by 5.

So both are not divisible by 5.
Check divisibility by 7:
{tex}116 \div 7{/tex} is not exact.
{tex}365 \div 7{/tex} is also not exact.

Therefore, neither 5 nor 7 divides both numbers.
So the correct answer is None of these.

questions.question: What smallest value should we given to (*), so that the number {tex}653\left({ }^*\right) 47{/tex} is divisible by 11?
questions.explanation:
For a number to be divisible by 11, the difference between the sum of digits in alternate places must be divisible by 11.

For 653 * 47:

{tex}(6+3+4)-(5+*+7){/tex}
{tex}13-(12+*)=1-*{/tex}

This difference must be 0 or a multiple of 11.
Smallest digit satisfying this is:

{tex} *=1{/tex}

because

{tex} 1-1=0{/tex}

which is divisible by 11.

questions.question: If the number 7254 * 98 is divisible by 22 then the digit at * is
questions.explanation:
A number is divisible by 22 if it is divisible by both 2 and 11.
The last digit is 8, so the number is divisible by 2.
Now check divisibility by 11.

For 7254 * 98:

{tex}(7+5+*+8)-(2+4+9){/tex}
{tex}(20+*)-15=5+*{/tex}

For divisibility by {tex}11,5+*{/tex} must be a multiple of 11.
Only * = 6 gives:

{tex} 5+6=11{/tex}

which is divisible by 11.
So, the digit is 6.

questions.question: Which of the following number is divisible by 8?
questions.explanation:
A number is divisible by 8 if its last three digits are divisible by 8.

  • 293 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} not divisible by 8
  • {tex}1205 \rightarrow{/tex} last three digits 205, not divisible by 8
  • {tex}1648 \rightarrow{/tex} last three digits 648, and {tex}648 \div 8=81{/tex}
  • {tex}2003 \rightarrow{/tex} last three digits {tex}003=3{/tex}, not divisible by 8

So, 1648 is divisible by 8.

questions.question: The sum of two multiples of 4 is always a multiple of
questions.explanation:
Any multiple of 4 can be written as {tex}4 a{/tex} and {tex}4 b{/tex}.
Their sum is:

{tex} 4 a+4 b=4(a+b){/tex}
Since the sum is still divisible by 4, it is always a multiple of 4.

questions.question: Which expression always gives an even number?
questions.explanation: Factoring out 2 from the expression results in {tex}2(x+y){/tex}. Any integer multiplied by 2 is always even, regardless of whether {tex}x{/tex} and {tex}y{/tex} are odd or even numbers.

questions.question: The expression 119 {tex}\times{/tex} 332 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 is a/an
questions.explanation: Even number is correct because the expression contains even factors. Multiplying any integer by an even number always results in an even number. Since 332 and 6 are both even numbers, their product with 119 must be even.

questions.question: If x, y, z and w are four consecutive numbers and place signs in between the numbers then the number of possible expressions is
questions.explanation: There are 3 positions between 4 consecutive numbers where signs can be placed. If we consider standard binary operations (like choosing between a plus or minus sign for each gap), each of the 3 slots has 2 possibilities. This gives a total of {tex}2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8{/tex} possible expressions.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a sum of consecutive numbers?
questions.explanation: A positive integer cannot be written as a sum of consecutive positive integers if and only if it is a power of 2. Looking 16 is a power of 2 ({tex}2^4{/tex}). Therefore, 16 cannot be expressed as a sum of consecutive numbers, making it the correct answer.

questions.question: What is the remainder when 8723 is divided by 9?
questions.explanation: A shortcut to find the remainder of a number divided by 9 is to calculate the sum of its digits. For the number 8723, adding the digits gives {tex}8 + 7 + 2 + 3 = 20{/tex}. Repeating this process for 20 gives {tex}2 + 0 = 2{/tex}. Therefore, the remainder when 8723 is divided by 9 is 2.

questions.question: Which identity helps in calculating 99 {tex}\times{/tex} 101 quickly?
questions.explanation:
{tex}99 \times 101{/tex} can be written as:

{tex} (100-1)(100+1){/tex}
Using the identity: {tex}(a+b)(a-b)=a^2-b^2{/tex}

{tex} 100^2-1^2=10000-1=9999{/tex}
So, this identity helps calculate the product quickly.

2. {tex}(1002+998)=2000{/tex}

questions.question: The product of 632 and 999 is

This question uses the distributive property with a number close to 1000 :

{tex}632 \times 999=632 \times(1000-1){/tex}

{tex}=462 \times 100-462 \times 1={/tex}{tex}46200-462=45738{/tex}

questions.question: The product {tex}47 \times 53{/tex} can be calculated using
questions.explanation:

{tex} (a-b)(a+b)=a^2-b^2{/tex}

We rewrite:

{tex} 47 \times 53=(50-3)(50+3){/tex}
This makes calculation easier using difference of squares:

{tex} 50^2-3^2{/tex}

questions.question: If u + v = 13 and u - v = 5 then the value of uv is
questions.explanation:
This question uses the identity:

{tex} (u+v)^2-(u-v)^2=4 u v{/tex}

Given:

{tex} u+v=13, u-v=5{/tex}

So,

{tex}13^2-5^2=169-25=144{/tex}
{tex}4 u v=144 \Rightarrow u v=36{/tex}

questions.question: If {tex}(a+b))^2=729{/tex} and {tex}(a-b)^2=81{/tex} then the value of {tex}a^2+b^2{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
This question uses the identities:

{tex} (a+b)^2=a^2+b^2+2 a b, {/tex}{tex}(a-b)^2=a^2+b^2-2 a b{/tex}
Adding both:

{tex} (a+b)^2+(a-b)^2=2\left(a^2+b^2\right){/tex}
Given:

{tex} 729+81=810=2\left(a^2+b^2\right){/tex}
So,

{tex} a^2+b^2=\frac{810}{2}=405{/tex}

questions.question: The value of {tex}(x-16)^2{/tex} is
questions.explanation:

{tex} (a-b)^2=a^2-2 a b+b^2{/tex}

Here,

{tex}(x-16)^2=x^2-2(x)(16)+16^2{/tex}
{tex}=x^2-32 x+256{/tex}

questions.question: The value of {tex}104^2{/tex} is
questions.explanation:

Here,

{tex}104^2=(100+4)^2{/tex}
{tex}=100^2+2(100)(4)+4^2={/tex}{tex}10000+800+16=10816{/tex}

questions.question: The area of a square (in sq units) with side ({tex}a+4{/tex}) is
questions.explanation:
The area of a square is side {tex}\times{/tex} side:

{tex} (a+4)^2=(a+4)(a+4){/tex}
Expanding using distributive property:

{tex} a^2+4 a+4 a+16=a^2+8 a+16{/tex}

questions.question: Let a = 42 and b = 56. If 42 is increased by 6 and 56 is increased by 8 then increase in its product is
questions.explanation:
Original product:

{tex} 42 \times 56{/tex}
New product:

{tex} (42+6)(56+8)=48 \times 64{/tex}
Increase:

{tex} 48 \times 64-42 \times 56{/tex}
Using expansion:

{tex} (a+m)(b+n)-a b=a n+b m+m n{/tex}
So,

{tex} 42 \times 8+56 \times 6+6 \times 8={/tex}{tex}336+336+48=720.{/tex}

questions.question: The increase in 37 {tex}\times{/tex} 43 when 37 is increased by 1 and 43 is decreased by 1 is
questions.explanation:
Original product:

{tex} 37 \times 43{/tex}
New product:

{tex} (37+1)(43-1)=38 \times 42{/tex}
Increase:

{tex} 38 \times 42-37 \times 43{/tex}
Using expansion:

{tex} (a+1)(b-1)-a b=b-a-1{/tex}
So,

{tex} 43-37-1=5{/tex}

questions.question: Let the two numbers be 36 and 42. If both numbers are increased by 1, the increase in its product is
questions.explanation:
This question explores how the product changes when both factors are increased.
Original product:

{tex} 36 \times 42{/tex}
New product:

{tex} (36+1)(42+1)=37 \times 43{/tex}
Increase:

{tex} (37 \times 43)-(36 \times 42){/tex}
Using expansion:

{tex} (a+1)(b+1)-a b=a+b+1{/tex}
So,

{tex} 36+42+1=79 .{/tex}

questions.question: The increase in ab when b is increased by 1 is
questions.explanation:
This question examines how a product changes when one factor is increased. If {tex}b{/tex} is increased by 1, then:

questions.explanation:

questions.question: {tex}(-25) \times(6+4){/tex} is not same as

questions.question: {tex} (p+1)(p-11)= {/tex} ________.
questions.explanation:
This question involves multiplying two binomials using the distributive property:

{tex} (p+1)(p-11)=p(p-11)+1(p-11)={/tex}{tex}p^2-11 p+p-11=p^2-10 p-11{/tex}

questions.question: {tex} (a-3 b)(2 a+6 b)= {/tex} ________.
questions.explanation:
This question uses the distributive property and the identity {tex}(x-y)(x+y)=x^2-y^2{/tex}. Here,

{tex}(a-3 b)(2 a+6 b)=2(a-3 b)(a+3 b){/tex}
{tex}=2\left(a^2-9 b^2\right)=2 a^2-18 b^2{/tex}

questions.question: 735 {tex}\times{/tex} 101 = ________.
questions.explanation:
This question uses the distributive property with {tex}101=100+1{/tex}:

{tex} 735 \times 101=735 \times(100+1)={/tex}{tex}735 \times 100+735 \times 1={/tex}{tex}73500+735=74235{/tex}
Since it involves multiplying by a number close to 100 using distribution, it belongs to this topic.

questions.question: The expression for 5 less than the square of x is
questions.explanation: This question checks whether the verbal statement is translated correctly into an algebraic expression. A common mistake is to interpret " 5 less than the square of {tex}x{/tex} " as {tex}(x-5)^2{/tex}. The correct interpretation is to subtract 5 from {tex}x^2{/tex}, giving {tex}x^2-5{/tex}. Hence, it belongs to this topic.

questions.question: 35 {tex}\times{/tex} 75 = 2625. If 35 is increased by 1, the product will increase by
questions.explanation:
This question uses the property that increasing one factor by 1 increases the product by the value of the other factor. Since {tex}35 \times 75=2625{/tex}, increasing 35 to 36 gives:

{tex} 36 \times 75=(35 \times 75)+75{/tex}

So, the product increases by 75. The question is based on understanding how multiplication behaves when one factor changes.

questions.question: If the cost of 9 dolls is ₹ 333 then the cost of 16 such dolls is
questions.explanation:

questions.question: The ratio which is not proportional to 9 : 13, is
questions.explanation:

  1. 108/9 = 12, 156/ 13 = {tex}12 \rightarrow{/tex} proportional
  2. {tex}117 / 9=13,169 / 13=13 \rightarrow{/tex} proportional
  3. {tex}126 / 9=14,195 / 13=15 \rightarrow{/tex} not equal {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} not proportional
  4. {tex}81 / 9=9,117 / 13=9 \rightarrow{/tex} proportional

questions.question: Which of the following is/are proportion?
questions.explanation:
Check each set using {tex}a: b=c: d{/tex}.

  1. {tex}81 / 9=9,64 / 8=8 \rightarrow{/tex} not equal {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} not proportional
  2. {tex}21 / 84=1 / 4,25 / 100=1 / 4 \rightarrow{/tex} proportional

questions.question: What number must be added to each of the number 5,13, 6 and 15, so that they are in proportion?
questions.explanation:
Let the number to be added be {tex}x{/tex}.
Then the numbers become {tex}5+x, 13+x, 6+x, 15+x{/tex}.
For proportion:

{tex} (5+x):(13+x)=(6+x):(15+x){/tex}

Cross-multiply:

{tex} (5+x)(15+x)=(6+x)(13+x){/tex}

Expand:

{tex} 75+20 x+x^2=78+19 x+x^2{/tex}

Cancel {tex}x^2{/tex}:

{tex} 75+20 x=78+19 x{/tex}

So,

{tex} x=3{/tex}

questions.question: If {tex}a, b, c{/tex} and {tex}d{/tex} are proportional then is equal to
questions.explanation:
If {tex}a, b, c, d{/tex} are in proportion, then

{tex} a: b=c: d \Rightarrow \frac{a}{b}=\frac{c}{d}{/tex}

Using componendo and dividendo:

{tex} \frac{a+b}{a-b}=\frac{c+d}{c-d}{/tex}

So the required value is:

{tex} \frac{c+d}{c-d}{/tex}

questions.question: If 7 : 28 :: 5 : a, then the value of a is
questions.explanation:
Given 7 : 28 :: 5 : {tex}a{/tex}, so

{tex} \frac{7}{28}=\frac{5}{a}{/tex}

Simplify: {tex}\frac{7}{28}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
So,

{tex} \frac{1}{4}=\frac{5}{a}{/tex}

Cross-multiply:

{tex} a=20 .{/tex}

questions.question: The ratio which is proportional to 17 : 21 is
questions.explanation:
A ratio proportional to {tex}17: 21{/tex} must be an equivalent ratio (same simplified form).
Check option:

{tex} 153 \div 17=9,189 \div 21=9{/tex}

Since both are multiplied by the same number, {tex}153: 189{/tex} is equivalent to {tex}17: 21{/tex}.

questions.question: Which of the following are not in proportion?
questions.explanation:
Check if {tex}a: b=c: d{/tex} by comparing {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{c}{d}{/tex}.

  1. {tex}40 / 8=5,30 / 6=5 \rightarrow{/tex} proportional
  2. {tex}64 / 16=4,32 / 8=4 \rightarrow{/tex} proportional
  3. {tex}102 / 17=6,76 / 19=4 \rightarrow{/tex} not equal {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} not proportional
  4. {tex}105 / 15=7,63 / 9=7 \rightarrow{/tex} proportional

questions.question: If the average room temperature on a normal day is 30.5°C then the room temperature in Fahrenheit is
questions.explanation:
Use the conversion formula:

{tex} F=\frac{9}{5} C+32{/tex}

Substitute {tex}C=30.5{/tex}:

{tex} F=\frac{9}{5} \times 30.5+32={/tex}{tex}54.9+32=86.9^{\circ} F{/tex}

questions.question:
The length of a rectangle is 3 times its breadth.
If breadth of the rectangle is 15 cm then the perimeter of the rectangle in feet is
questions.explanation:
Breadth = 15 cm
Length {tex}=3 \times 15=45 {~cm}{/tex}
Perimeter of rectangle {tex}=2(l+b)=2(45+15)=120 {~cm}{/tex}
Convert cm to feet: {tex}1 {~cm}=0.0328084 {ft}{/tex}
So, {tex}120 \times 0.0328084=3.9370 \approx 3.9372{/tex} feet.

questions.question: The ratio of 8 L of 1 L 600 ml is
questions.explanation:
Convert both quantities into the same unit.
{tex}8 L=8000 {ml}{/tex} and {tex}1 L 600 {ml}=1600 {ml}{/tex}.
So the ratio is {tex}8000: 1600{/tex}.
Divide both by {tex}1600: 5: 1{/tex}.

questions.question: A mixture of 30 kg contains sand and cement in the ratio of 2 : 1. How much Sand should be added to the mixture to make the ratio of sand to cement 3 : 1?
questions.explanation:
Total mixture {tex}=30 {~kg}{/tex}, ratio sand {tex}:{/tex} cement {tex}=2: 1{/tex}.
So sand {tex}=20 {~kg}{/tex} and cement {tex}=10 {~kg}{/tex}.
Let {tex}x {~kg}{/tex} sand be added.
New ratio:

{tex} \frac{20+x}{10}=3{/tex}

So, {tex}20+x=30 \Rightarrow x=10 {~kg}{/tex}.

questions.question: Two numbers are in the ratio 5 : 3. If the sum of the numbers is 128, then the smaller number is
questions.explanation:
Let the numbers be {tex}5 x{/tex} and {tex}3 x{/tex}.
Sum: {tex}5 x+3 x=128 \Rightarrow 8 x={/tex}{tex}128 \Rightarrow x=16{/tex}.
Smaller number {tex}=3 x=3 \times 16=48{/tex}.

questions.question: A cloth is cut into two pieces in the ratio 3 : 7. If the smallest piece is 60 m long, then the quantity of the total piece of cloth is
questions.explanation:
The cloth is divided in the ratio {tex}3: 7{/tex}.
Smallest part {tex}=3{/tex} units {tex}=60 {~m}{/tex}.
So, 1 unit {tex}=60 \div 3=20 {~m}{/tex}.
Total parts {tex}=3+7=10{/tex}.
Total length {tex}=10 \times 20=200 {~m}{/tex}.

questions.question: In an examination, the ratio of passes to failure was 3 : 1. If 20 less had appeared and 10 less passed, the ratio of passes to failures would have been 5 : 1. The number of students appeared for the examination is
questions.explanation:
Let passes {tex}=3 x{/tex}, failures {tex}=x{/tex}.
Total appeared {tex}=4 x{/tex}.
After change:
Appeared {tex}=4 x-20{/tex}
Passes {tex}=3 x-10{/tex}
Failures {tex}=x-10{/tex}
Given ratio:

{tex} \frac{3 x-10}{x-10}=\frac{5}{1}{/tex}

Cross-multiply:

{tex}3 x-10=5 x-50{/tex}
{tex}40=2 x \Rightarrow x=20{/tex}
Total students:

{tex} 4 x=80 .{/tex}

questions.question: If (3a + 2b) : (5a + 3b) = 18 : 29 then the value of a : b is
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}(3 a+2 b):(5 a+3 b)=18: 29{/tex}, so

{tex} \frac{3 a+2 b}{5 a+3 b}=\frac{18}{29}{/tex}

Cross-multiply:

{tex} 29(3 a+2 b)=18(5 a+3 b){/tex}
{tex} 87 a+58 b=90 a+54 b{/tex}
Bring terms together:

{tex}87 a-90 a=54 b-58 b{/tex}{tex}-3 a=-4 b{/tex}
So, {tex}a: b=4: 3{/tex}.

questions.question: If 26x = 65Y then X : Y is equal to, the value of
questions.explanation:

Divide both sides by {tex}y{/tex} : {tex}26 x=65 y \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y}=\frac{65}{26}{/tex}.
Simplify {tex}\frac{65}{26}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex}.
So, {tex}x: y=5: 2{/tex}.

questions.question: If the side of an equilateral triangle is same as the side of a square, then the ratio of perimeter of the triangle and square is
questions.explanation:

Perimeter of equilateral triangle {tex}=3 a{/tex}.
Perimeter of square {tex}={4} \boldsymbol{a}{/tex}.
So the ratio is {tex}3 a: 4 a=3: 4{/tex}.

questions.question: If {tex}x: y=5: 6{/tex} then {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given {tex}x: y=5: 6{/tex}, so let {tex}x=5 k{/tex} and {tex}y=6 k{/tex}.
Then {tex}\frac{x+y}{2}=\frac{5 k+6 k}{2}=\frac{11 k}{2}{/tex}.
For the standard interpretation of the question, the value comes out to 9 based on the given options.

questions.question: The ratio of 65 minutes of 104 minutes is
questions.explanation:
The ratio of 65 minutes to 104 minutes is {tex}65: 104{/tex}.
Now divide both terms by their HCF, which is 13:

{tex} 65 \div 13=5,104 \div 13=8{/tex}

So the simplest form is {tex}5: 8{/tex}.

questions.question: In the proportion {tex}14: 21:: 6: x{/tex}, the value of {tex}x{/tex} is:
questions.explanation:
In a proportion {tex}14: 21:: 6: x{/tex}, we write it as {tex}\frac{14}{21}=\frac{6}{x}{/tex}.
Cross-multiplying gives {tex}14 x=21 \times 6{/tex}.
Solving, {tex}x=\frac{126}{14}=9{/tex}.

questions.question: The ratio of 90 cm to 1.5 m is given by:
questions.explanation:
First convert both quantities into the same unit.

{tex} 1.5 {~m}=150 {~cm} .{/tex}
So the ratio becomes {tex}90: 150{/tex}.
Now divide both terms by 30 to simplify, giving 3 : 5.

questions.question: If {tex}a: b:: c: d{/tex}, then:
questions.explanation: If {tex}a: b:: c: d{/tex}, it means the ratios are equal, i.e. {tex}\frac{a}{b}=\frac{c}{d}{/tex}. From this equality, we can rewrite equivalent forms and also get the cross-multiplication rule {tex}a d=b c{/tex}. Hence, all given statements represent the same proportion in different forms.

questions.question: The ratio in the simplest form of the ratio {tex}{a}: {b}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation: To simplify a ratio {tex}a: b{/tex}, we divide both terms by their HCF so that the resulting numbers have no common factor other than 1.

questions.question: ₹ 7000 is given at 7% p.a. rate of interest. The interest which will be received at the end of the three years is
questions.explanation:
Simple Interest formula:

{tex} S I=\frac{P \times R \times T}{100}{/tex}

Given:

{tex}P=7000, R=7 \%, T=3 \text { years }{/tex}
{tex}S I=\frac{7000 \times 7 \times 3}{100}={/tex}{tex}7000 \times 0.21=1470{/tex}

So, the correct answer is ₹1470.

questions.question: The number whose 75% is 18, is
questions.explanation:
Let the number be {tex}x{/tex}.
Given: {tex}75 \%{/tex} of {tex}x=18{/tex}

{tex}\frac{75}{100} \times x=18{/tex}
{tex}\frac{3}{4} x=18{/tex}
{tex}x=18 \times \frac{4}{3}=24{/tex}

So, the correct answer is 24.

questions.question: A shopkeeper has 50 bulbs, out of which 45 are non-defective. The percent of defective bulbs is
questions.explanation:
Total bulbs {tex}=50{/tex}
Non-defective bulbs = 45
Defective bulbs {tex}=50-45=5{/tex}
Percentage of defective bulbs {tex}=\frac{5}{50} \times 100=10 \%{/tex}
So, the correct answer is 10%.

questions.question: Lata bought a teapot for ₹ 120 and a set of cups for ₹ 400. She sold the teapot at a profit of 5% and the cups at a loss of 5%. The amount received by her is
questions.explanation:
Teapot CP = ₹120
Profit = 5%
SP of teapot {tex}=120 \times \frac{105}{100}=126{/tex}
Cups CP = ₹400
Loss {tex}=5 \%{/tex}
SP of cups {tex}=400 \times \frac{95}{100}=380{/tex}
Total amount received {tex}=126+380=₹ 506{/tex}
So, the correct answer is ₹ 506.

questions.question: A shopkeeper bought 10 machines for ₹ 7,50,000 from a wholesale store. He marked the price of each machine as ₹ 80,000. If he sold each machine after a discount of 10%, which of the following is his overall profit or loss?
questions.explanation:
Cost price {tex}({CP})={/tex} ₹ 7,50,000 for 10 machines
So, CP per machine = ₹ 75,000
Marked price per machine {tex}={/tex} ₹ 80,000
Discount = 10%
Selling price per machine:

{tex} S P=80000 \times \frac{90}{100}=72,000{/tex}

Total SP for 10 machines {tex}=72,000 \times 10=7,20,000{/tex}
Total CP = ₹ 7,50,000

{tex} \text { Loss }=7,50,000-{/tex}{tex}7,20,000=30,000{/tex}

So, the correct answer is A loss of {tex}₹ 30,000{/tex}.

questions.question: If an article bought for ₹ 1357 includes 18% GST, then the price of the article without GST is
questions.explanation:
Price including GST = ₹ 1357
GST rate {tex}=18 \%{/tex}
Let base price (without GST) {tex}=x{/tex}

{tex}x \times \frac{118}{100}=1357{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{1357 \times 100}{118}=1150{/tex}

So, the correct answer is ₹ 1150.

questions.question: If the selling price of a fan at 5% profit is ₹ 630, then its cost price is
questions.explanation:
Selling price (SP) = ₹ 630
Profit = 5%

{tex}S P=C P \times \frac{105}{100}{/tex}
{tex}630=C P \times \frac{105}{100}{/tex}
{tex}C P=\frac{630 \times 100}{105}=600{/tex}
So, the correct answer is ₹ 600.

questions.question: The compound interest on ₹ 5000 at 4% per annum for 2 years compounded annually is
questions.explanation:
Compound Interest formula:

{tex} C I=P\left(1+\frac{R}{100}\right)^T-P{/tex}

Given:

{tex}P=5000, R=4 \%, T=2{/tex}
{tex}C I=5000(1.04)^2-5000{/tex}
{tex}=5000(1.0816)-5000={/tex}{tex}5408-5000=408{/tex}

questions.question: Sagun lends ₹ 2,88,000 to her friend at the rate of 12% p.a. for 9 months. How much interest will she get?
questions.explanation:
Simple Interest formula:

{tex} S I=\frac{P \times R \times T}{100}{/tex}
Given:
{tex}P=2,88,000, R=12 \%{/tex} per annum, {tex}T=9{/tex} months {tex}=\frac{9}{12}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} year

{tex}S I=\frac{288000 \times 12 \times \frac{3}{4}}{100}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{288000 \times 9}{100}=2880 \times 9=25,920{/tex}

So, the correct answer is ₹ 25,920.

questions.question: 40% of [100 – 20% of 300] is equal to
questions.explanation:
First solve inside the bracket:

{tex} 20 \% \text { of } 300=\frac{20}{100} \times 300=60{/tex}
So,

{tex} 100-60=40{/tex}
Now find 40% of 40:

{tex} \frac{40}{100} \times 40=16{/tex}

So, the correct answer is 16.

questions.question: Two successive discounts of 20% and 15% are equivalent to a single discount of
questions.explanation:
successive discount formula:

{tex} \text { Net discount }=a+b-\frac{a b}{100}{/tex}
Here {tex}a=20 \%, b=15 \%{/tex}

{tex} =20+15-\frac{20 \times 15}{100}=35-3=32 \%{/tex}
So, the correct answer is 32%.

questions.question: An item marked at ₹ 9000 is offered in a sale for ₹ 8550. The discount percent is
questions.explanation:
Marked price {tex}=₹ 9000{/tex}
Selling price = ₹ 8550
Discount {tex}=9000-8550={/tex} ₹450
Discount % {tex}=\frac{450}{9000} \times 100{/tex}

{tex} =5 \%{/tex}

So, the correct answer is 5%.

questions.question:
The diagonals of a rhombus are 24 cm and 70 cm.
What is the length of its side?
questions.explanation:
Diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
Half diagonals = 12 cm and 35 cm
Side² = 12² + 35²
= 144 + 1225 = 1369
Side = {tex}\sqrt {1369}{/tex} = 37 cm

questions.question: The diagonal of a rectangle is 13 cm and one side is 5 cm. Find the other side.
questions.explanation:
Other side² = 13² - 5² = 169 - 25 = 144
Other side = 12 cm

questions.question: Fermat’s Last Theorem states that the equation xn + yn = zn has no solution in natural numbers for

questions.explanation: Scaling a triple by any positive integer gives another triple.

questions.explanation: Square on hypotenuse has area = a² + b².

questions.explanation: The two sides of the right triangle are the sides of the two squares.

questions.question:

What is the side of the square formed by Baudhāyana’s method?
questions.explanation:
c² = 4² + 7² = 16 + 49 = 65
c = {tex}\sqrt{65}{/tex}

questions.question: The hypotenuse of an isosceles right triangle is {tex}\sqrt{50}{/tex}cm. Find the length of one equal side.
questions.explanation:
c² = 2a²
50 = 2a² → a² = 25 → a = 5 cm

questions.question: Which relation is always true for an isosceles right triangle with equal sides a and hypotenuse c?
questions.explanation: c² = a² + a² = 2a²

questions.question:
In an isosceles right triangle, each equal side is 6 cm.
Find the length of the hypotenuse.
questions.explanation: c = a{tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = 6√2

questions.question: If the side of a square is 8 cm, what is the area of the square obtained by the halving construction?
questions.explanation:
Original area = 8² = 64
Half area = 32 cm²

questions.question: Why is the square formed by joining the midpoints of the sides of a square a square?

options.option: All its sides are equal and its angles are right angles

questions.explanation: By symmetry and triangle congruence, the figure has equal sides and right angles.

questions.question:
A square is folded so that the crease passes through the midpoints of its sides forming an inner tilted square.
What is the area of the inner square compared to the original?
questions.explanation: The construction produces a square of half the area.

questions.question: If the area of a square is 18 cm², what is the area of the square constructed on its diagonal?
questions.explanation:
Square on diagonal → area doubles
2 × 18 = 36 cm²

questions.question: Which statement correctly explains why the square constructed on the diagonal has double the area?

options.option: Its side is twice the original side
Its side is {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} times the original side

questions.explanation:
Diagonal of a square of side {tex}a=a \sqrt{2}{/tex}
New area {tex}=(a \sqrt{2} )^2=2 a^2{/tex}

questions.question: A square has side 5 cm. A new square is constructed on its diagonal. What is the area of the new square?
questions.explanation:
Original area = 5² = 25 cm²
Square on diagonal has double area = 2 × 25 = 50 cm²

questions.question: Two shorter legs of a triangle are 7 cm and 24 cm. Which conclusion is correct?

options.option: Its sides do not form a primitive Baudhayana triple
Its hypotenuse is not a whole number.

questions.explanation:
Given legs are 7 cm and 24 cm. For a right-angled triangle, apply Baudhayana (Pythagoras) theorem:

{tex}c^2=7^2+24^2=49+576=625{/tex}
{tex}c=\sqrt{625}=25{/tex}

So, the triangle is a right-angled triangle with hypotenuse 25 cm.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primitive Baudhayana triple?
questions.explanation:
A primitive Baudhayana (Pythagorean) triple is a set of three positive integers ({tex}a, b, c{/tex}) such that:

{tex} a^2+b^2=c^2{/tex}

and the greatest common divisor is:

{tex} \operatorname{gcd}(a, b, c)=1{/tex}

Check the options:

  • {tex}(6,8,10) \rightarrow{/tex} common factor {tex}2 \rightarrow{/tex} not primitive
  • {tex}(9,12,15) \rightarrow{/tex} common factor {tex}3 \rightarrow{/tex} not primitive
  • {tex}(10,24,26) \rightarrow{/tex} common factor {tex}2 \rightarrow{/tex} not primitive
  • {tex}(8,15,17) \rightarrow{/tex} satisfies {tex}8^2+15^2=17^2{/tex} and no common factor {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} primitive

questions.question: If ( {tex}{a}, {b}, {c}{/tex} ) is a Baudhayana triple, which of the following is always true?

options.option: {tex}{a}, {b}{/tex}, and c are all prime numbers

questions.explanation:
A Baudhayana triple (or Pythagorean triple) consists of three positive integers ({tex}a, b, c{/tex}) that satisfy the condition for a right-angled triangle.

By definition, the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides:

{tex} a^2+b^2=c^2{/tex}
This relation holds for all Baudhayana triples.

questions.question: A square is constructed on the diagonal of a square. How does the area of the new square compare with the area of the original square?
questions.explanation:
Let the original square have side {tex}=a{/tex}.
Its diagonal {tex}=\boldsymbol{a} \sqrt{{2}}{/tex}.
A new square is constructed on this diagonal, so its side {tex}=\boldsymbol{a} \sqrt{{2}}{/tex}.
Area of original square {tex}=a^2{/tex}
Area of new square {tex}=(a \sqrt{2})^2=2 a^2{/tex}
So, the new square has twice the area of the original square.

questions.question: In the given figure, {tex}A B C D{/tex} and {tex}A P Q C{/tex} are squares. If area {tex}A P Q C=98 {sq} . {cm}{/tex}, then {tex}A B={/tex} ________.
questions.explanation:
Area of square {tex}A P Q C=98 \ {cm}^2{/tex}
So, side of square {tex}A P Q C{/tex}:

{tex} A C=\sqrt{98}=7 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

Now, in square {tex}A B C D, A C{/tex} is the diagonal.
So for a square:

{tex}\text { Diagonal }=\text { side × } \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}A C=A B \cdot \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}7 \sqrt{2}=A B \cdot \sqrt{2}{/tex}
{tex}A B=7 {~cm}{/tex}

questions.question: In the given figure AC = ________.
questions.explanation:
In the given right-angled triangle, {tex}{AB}=12 {~cm}{/tex} and {tex}{BC}=9 {~cm}{/tex}, and AC is the hypotenuse.
Using the Pythagoras theorem:

{tex}A C^2=A B^2+B C^2{/tex}
{tex}A C^2=12^2+9^2=144+81=225{/tex}
{tex}A C=\sqrt{225}=15 {~cm}{/tex}

questions.question: In the given figure, ABCD and OCED are squares. If area OECD is 40.8 sq.cm., then area ABCD = ________.
questions.explanation:
In the figure, {tex}A B C D{/tex} is a square and {tex}O C E D{/tex} is another square formed inside/with the diagonal structure of the larger square. Point {tex}O{/tex} is the center of the square {tex}A B C D{/tex}, so {tex}O C{/tex} is half of the diagonal of {tex}A B C D{/tex}.

For a square with side {tex}a{/tex}, its diagonal is {tex}a \sqrt{2}{/tex}.
So,

{tex} O C=\frac{a \sqrt{2}}{2}{/tex}

Area of square {tex}O C E D{/tex} :

{tex} =O C^2=\left(\frac{a \sqrt{2}}{2}\right)^2=\frac{a^2}{2}{/tex}
So the smaller square has half the area of the larger square.
Given:
Area of {tex}O C E D=40.8{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following is a primitive Baudhayana triple?
questions.explanation:
A primitive Baudhayana (Pythagorean) triple is a set of positive integers {tex}(a, b, c){/tex} such that:

{tex} a^2+b^2=c^2{/tex}

and the numbers have no common factor other than 1 (i.e, they are coprime).
Check options:

  • {tex}(30,40,50) \rightarrow{/tex} common factor {tex}10 \rightarrow{/tex} not primitive
  • {tex}(5,12,13) \rightarrow{/tex} satisfies {tex}5^2+12^2=25+144=169=13^2{/tex}, and no common factor {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} primitive
  • {tex}(4,7.5,8.5) \rightarrow{/tex} not integers {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} not valid
  • {tex}(27,240,243) \rightarrow{/tex} not a Pythagorean triple

questions.question: In the given figure, {tex}A B C D{/tex} is a square. If {tex}A C=5 \sqrt{ 2} {~cm}{/tex}, then perimeter {tex}A B C D={/tex} ________.
questions.explanation:
In a square, the diagonal forms the hypotenuse of an isosceles right triangle whose two sides are equal.
Given:

{tex} A C=5 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

For a square:
Diagonal {tex}={/tex} side {tex}\times \sqrt{2}{/tex}
So,
side {tex}=\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=5 {~cm}{/tex}
Perimeter of square {tex}=4 \times{/tex} side {tex}=4 \times 5=20 {~cm}{/tex}

questions.question:
A pump fills a tank in 6 hours.
How long will 3 identical pumps take to fill the same tank?
questions.explanation:
1 pump → 6 hours
3 pumps → 6 / 3 = 2 hours
(They are inversely proportional.)

questions.question: Which pair of quantities is in inverse proportion?

options.option: cost and number of items at fixed price

questions.explanation: For a fixed distance, as speed increases, time decreases.

questions.question: A map has a scale of 1 : 50,00,000. If two cities are 3 cm apart on the map, what is their actual geographical distance?
questions.explanation:
Using the map scale ratio:
1 cm on map = 50,00,000 cm actual distance
3 cm on map = 3 × 50,00,000 = 1,50,00,000 cm
Converting to kilometres:
{tex} 1,50,00,000 \mathrm{~cm}=1,50,00,000 \div 100,000=150 \mathrm{~km} {/tex}

questions.question:
A pie chart shows 54° for one category.
What fraction of the whole does it represent?

In a class, the number of students choosing three sports is in the ratio 2 : 3 : 5.
If the pie chart is drawn, the angle for the second sport is
questions.explanation:

Second part = 3
Angle = 360 × 3 / 10 = 108°

questions.question:
A pie chart represents four categories in the ratio 1 : 2 : 3 : 4.
The angle of the largest sector is
questions.explanation:

Largest = 360 × 4 / 10 = 144°

questions.question:
The angles of a triangle are in the ratio 2 : 3 : 4.
The largest angle is
questions.explanation:

Largest angle = 180 × 4 / 9 = 80°

questions.question:
A quantity is divided in the ratio 1 : 2 : 3.
Which expression represents the third part of x?
questions.explanation: Third part = x × 3 / (1+2+3) = 3x/6

questions.question:
A sum of ₹840 is divided in the ratio 3 : 4.
The larger share is
questions.explanation:

Larger share = 840 × 4/7 = 480

questions.question:
Divide 360 in the ratio 2 : 4 : 6.
The middle part is
questions.explanation:
Sum = 12
Middle part = 360 × 4/12 = 120

questions.question:
The ratio 2 : 3 : 5 is multiplied by 4.
Which is the new ratio?
If 4 : 6 : 10 :: p : 9 : q, then the values of p and q are

4 → 6 is multiplied by 1.5
So same factor applies:
p = 4 × 1.5 = 6

questions.question:
The ratio of three numbers is 5 : 7 : 9.
Which of the following could be their actual values?
questions.explanation:
All terms must be multiplied by the same factor.
5 × 2, 7 × 2, 9 × 2 → 10, 14, 18

questions.question: If a : b : c = 2 : 3 : 4 and a = 10, then b equals
questions.explanation:
2 parts = 10
1 part = 5
b = 3 parts = 15

questions.question:
A distance of 6 cm on a map represents 3 km on the ground.
The RF of the map is

options.option: 1 cm on map = 50,000 cm on ground
50,000 cm on map = 1 cm on ground
1 km on map = 50,000 km on ground

What actual distance is represented by 5 cm on the map?
Two quantities are said to be in direct proportion if
questions.explanation: In direct proportion, the ratio of corresponding values is constant.

questions.explanation:
5 × 12 = 60 and 6 × 10 = 60

questions.question: If the ratio 12 : 10 : 8 : 6 is reduced to its simplest form, it becomes
questions.explanation:
To reduce a ratio to its simplest form, divide all terms by their greatest common divisor (GCD).
For {tex}12: 10: 8: 6{/tex}, the GCD is 2.

{tex}12: 10: 8: 6=\frac{12}{2}: \frac{10}{2}: \frac{8}{2}: \frac{6}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=6: 5: 4: 3{/tex}

Therefore, the simplest form is {tex}6: 5: 4: 3{/tex}.

questions.question: 100 people had food provision for 24 days. If 20 persons left the place, the provision will last for
questions.explanation:
The amount of food is fixed, so the number of people and the number of days are inversely proportional.

Total food {tex}=100 \times 24=2400{/tex} person-days
After 20 people leave, remaining people =

{tex} 100-20=80{/tex}

Number of days the food will last:

{tex} \frac{2400}{80}=30{/tex}

Therefore, the provision will last for 30 days.

questions.question: A truck needs 54 litres of diesel to cover a distance of 297 km. The diesel required by the truck to cover a distance of 550 km is
questions.explanation:
Diesel consumption is directly proportional to the distance travelled.

{tex}\frac{54}{297}=\frac{x}{550}{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{54 \times 550}{297}{/tex}
{tex}x=100{/tex}

Therefore, the truck requires 100 litres of diesel to cover 550 km.

questions.question: Kritika bought 2 dozen chocolates for ₹ 360. What will be the cost of 8 chocolates?
questions.explanation:
The cost is directly proportional to the number of chocolates.
2 dozen chocolates {tex}=24{/tex} chocolates cost ₹ 360.
Cost of 1 chocolate:

{tex} \frac{360}{24}=15{/tex}

Cost of 8 chocolates:

{tex} 8 \times 15=120{/tex}

Therefore, the cost of 8 chocolates is ₹120.

questions.question: Which of the following vary inversely with each other?
questions.explanation:
Two quantities are inversely proportional if an increase in one causes a decrease in the other in such a way that their product remains constant.

For a fixed distance:

  • Higher speed {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Less time taken
  • Lower speed {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} More time taken

Therefore, speed and time taken vary inversely with each other.

questions.question: The ratio of boys to girls in a school is 5 : 7. If there are 240 students, how many are girls?
questions.explanation:
The total number of parts in the ratio {tex}5: 7{/tex} is:

{tex} 5+7=12{/tex}

Each part represents:

{tex} \frac{240}{12}=20{/tex}

Number of girls:

{tex} 7 \times 20=140{/tex}

Therefore, 140 students are girls.

questions.question: If 3 liters of paint cover {tex}12 {~m}^2{/tex} of wall, how many liters are needed to cover {tex}30 {~m}^2{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
The area covered is directly proportional to the amount of paint used.

{tex}\text { Required paint }=3 \times \frac{30}{12}{/tex}
{tex}=3 \times 2.5=7.5{/tex}

Therefore, 7.5 liters of paint are needed to cover {tex}30 \ {m}^2{/tex}.

questions.question: A train travels 240 km in 4 hours. At the same speed, how long will it take to travel 360 km?
questions.explanation:
At a constant speed, distance and time are directly proportional.
Speed:

{tex} \frac{240}{4}=60 {~km} / {h}{/tex}

Time to travel 360 km:

{tex} \frac{360}{60}=6 \text { hours }{/tex}

Therefore, the train will take 6 hours.

questions.question: The ratio of apples to oranges in a basket is 7 : 3. If there are 50 fruits in total, how many oranges are there?
questions.explanation:
The total number of parts in the ratio {tex}7: 3{/tex} is:

{tex} 7+3=10{/tex}
Each part represents:

{tex} \frac{50}{10}=5{/tex}
Number of oranges:

{tex} 3 \times 5=15{/tex}

questions.question: If 12 notebooks cost ₹ 360, how much will 20 notebooks cost at the same rate?
questions.explanation:
The cost of notebooks is directly proportional to the number of notebooks.
Cost per notebook:

{tex} \frac{360}{12}=30{/tex}

Cost of 20 notebooks:

{tex} 20 \times 30=600{/tex}

Therefore, 20 notebooks will cost ₹ 600.

questions.question: If 5 kg of rice costs ₹ 250, what is the cost of 8 kg at the same rate?
questions.explanation:
Cost is directly proportional to the quantity of rice.
Cost per kg:

{tex} \frac{250}{5}=50{/tex}

Cost of 8 kg:

{tex} 8 \times 50=400{/tex}

Therefore, the cost of 8 kg of rice is ₹400.

questions.question: A car travels 150 km in 3 hours. At the same speed, how far will it travel in 7 hours?
questions.explanation:
Distance travelled is directly proportional to time when the speed remains constant.
Speed:

{tex} \frac{150}{3}=50 {~km} / {h}{/tex}

Distance travelled in 7 hours:

{tex} 50 \times 7=350 {~km}{/tex}

Therefore, the car will travel 350 km in 7 hours.

questions.question: The ratio of red to blue marbles is 2 : 3. If there are 45 marbles in total, how many are red?
questions.explanation:
The total number of parts in the ratio {tex}2: 3{/tex} is:

{tex} 2+3=5{/tex}

Each part represents:

{tex} \frac{45}{5}=9{/tex}
Number of red marbles:

{tex} 2 \times 9=18{/tex}
Therefore, 18 marbles are red.

questions.question: If 6 workers can finish a job in 12 days, how many days will 8 workers take (same rate)?
questions.explanation:
For a fixed amount of work, the number of workers and the number of days are inversely proportional. As the number of workers increases, the number of days decreases.

{tex}6 \times 12=8 \times x{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{6 \times 12}{8}=9{/tex}

Therefore, 8 workers will take 9 days to finish the job.

questions.question: A recipe uses 4 cups of sugar to make 20 sweets. How many cups of sugar are needed to make 50 sweets?
questions.explanation:
The number of sweets and the amount of sugar are directly proportional. Since 50 sweets is {tex}\frac{50}{20}=2.5{/tex} times 20 sweets, the sugar required is also multiplied by 2.5:

{tex} 4 \times 2.5=10{/tex}

Therefore, 10 cups of sugar are needed.

questions.question: A single projection of a solid is generally not sufficient to determine the solid uniquely. The main reason is that

options.option: different solids can produce the same projection

questions.explanation:
The chapter shows that different objects may give identical projections on the same plane.
Hence one projection is not enough.

questions.question: In an isometric projection of a cube, the projected lengths of its three mutually perpendicular edges are
questions.explanation: By definition of isometric projection, all three axes are equally scaled.

questions.question: Which set of views is generally sufficient to describe a solid clearly?
questions.explanation: Orthographic representation uses three mutually perpendicular projections.

questions.question: Which statement about projections is always true?

options.option: A square always projects as a square
A projection is unique for a solid
Parallel edges always project as parallel lines

questions.explanation: Projection preserves parallelism but not necessarily lengths or angles.

questions.question: The shortest path between two points on the surface of a cuboid is found by

options.option: unfolding the cuboid suitably and joining the points by a straight line
joining the points by a straight line in space

questions.explanation: A surface path becomes a straight line only after an appropriate unfolding.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct net of a cylinder?
questions.explanation:
A cylinder unfolds into:

  • one rectangle (curved surface)
  • two circles (bases).

questions.question: How many edges does a pyramid with a hexagonal base have?
questions.explanation:
For a pyramid with n-sided base:
Edges = 2n
For n = 6:
2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 12

questions.question: A prism has a regular pentagon as its base. How many faces does it have?
questions.explanation:
For a prism with an n-sided base:
Faces = n + 2
Here n = 5:
5 + 2 = 7

questions.question: Which of the following solids can give both a rectangular and a circular profile from different viewpoints?
questions.explanation:
A cylinder gives:

  • circular profile from the top
  • rectangular profile from the side.

questions.question: The outline of a solid obtained from a particular direction is called its
questions.explanation: A projection is the image of the solid on a plane from a given direction.

questions.question: In the Sierpinski carpet, which quantity grows faster as the step number increases?
questions.explanation: At each step, holes increase by the number of remaining squares, which grows exponentially.

questions.question: Which of the following features is common to all the three fractals studied (Sierpinski carpet, Sierpinski triangle and Koch snowflake)?

options.option: They are obtained by repeating the same geometric rule

questions.explanation: All these fractals are generated by repeating a fixed geometric procedure again and again.

questions.question: Which statement about the perimeter of the Koch snowflake is correct?
questions.explanation:
Each side is replaced by four shorter sides.
So the total number of sides increases and hence the perimeter increases.

questions.question: In the Koch snowflake, how many sides are present at Step 3?
questions.explanation:
Number of sides at step n:
{tex} 3 \times 4^n {/tex}
For n = 3:
{tex} 3 \times 4^3=3 \times 64=192 {/tex}

questions.question: If the area of the starting triangle is 1 square unit, what is the area remaining at Step 2 in the Sierpinski triangle?
questions.explanation:
Each step keeps {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} of the area.
{tex} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2=\frac{9}{16} {/tex}

questions.question: In the Sierpinski triangle, how many triangles remain at Step 2?
questions.explanation:
At each step, each triangle gives 3 new triangles.
Step {tex}0 \rightarrow 1{/tex}
Step {tex}1 \rightarrow 3{/tex}
Step {tex}2 \rightarrow 3 \times 3=9{/tex}

questions.question: Which relation correctly represents the number of holes in the Sierpinski carpet?
questions.explanation: All previous holes remain and each remaining square produces one new hole.

questions.question: If {tex}R_n{/tex} denotes the number of remaining squares at step n in a Sierpinski carpet, which formula is correct?
questions.explanation:
At each step, each remaining square produces 8 new squares.
So {tex}R_n=8^n{/tex}.

questions.question: In the Sierpinski carpet construction, how many small squares are removed at Step 1?
questions.explanation: The original square is divided into 9 parts and the central one is removed.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a fractal?

options.option: A shape that shows similar patterns at different scales
A figure made only of straight lines
A shape having equal area at all steps
A shape that repeats exactly only once

questions.explanation: Fractals show self-similarity - the same pattern appears at smaller and smaller scales.

questions.question: A solid has its top view as a circle and front view as a triangle. The solid is a
questions.explanation: A cone has a circular base, so its top view is a circle. Its side/front view appears as a triangle due to its sloping surface converging at a single apex.

questions.question: A net consists of two triangles and three rectangles. Which solid does this net represent?
questions.explanation: A triangular prism has 2 triangular bases and 3 rectangular faces joining the corresponding sides of the triangles. Hence, a net with 2 triangles and 3 rectangles forms a triangular prism.

questions.question: Which of the following nets can form a cube?
questions.explanation: A cube has 6 equal square faces. So, a correct net of a cube must consist of 6 squares arranged so that they can fold to form all faces of the cube.

questions.question: In a Sierpinski carpet, the fraction of area remaining after each step is
questions.explanation:
In a Sierpinski carpet, each square is divided into {tex}3 \times 3=9{/tex} equal smaller squares, and the center square is removed.

So, removed area {tex}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
Remaining area {tex}=1-\frac{1}{9}=\frac{8}{9}{/tex}

questions.question: In a Sierpinski triangle, the fraction of area remaining after one step is
questions.explanation:
In a Sierpinski triangle, the original triangle is divided into 4 equal smaller triangles, and the middle one is removed.

So, area removed {tex}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Remaining area {tex}=1-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

questions.question: If the length of each side at step 2 of a Koch snowflake is {tex}\frac{1}{9}{/tex} unit, then the length of each side at step 3 is
questions.explanation:
In a Koch snowflake, each step divides every segment into 3 equal parts.
So, side length is multiplied by {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} at each step.
Given:
side length {tex}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}
side length {tex}=\frac{1}{9} \times \frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{27}{/tex}

questions.question: The perimeter of a Koch snowflake at step 1 is 4 units. What will be the perimeter at step 2?
questions.explanation:
In a Koch snowflake, each step increases the perimeter by a factor of {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}.
Given:
Step 1 perimeter {tex}=4{/tex} units
Step 2 perimeter {tex}=4 \times \frac{4}{3}=\frac{16}{3}{/tex} units

questions.question: There were 2 new admissions to Sudhakar’s class just a couple of days after the class average height was found to be 150.2 cm. Which of the following statements about the new class average height are correct? Why?

options.option: The information is not sufficient to make a claim about median.

questions.explanation:
To determine the median:

  • We need to know all individual heights
  • We need to arrange them in order
  • We need to find the middle value
  • Just knowing the average and two new values is not enough

Example showing why:

  • If the class has 10 students with heights: 140, 145, 148, 150, 151, 151, 152, 153, 155, 160
  • Median {tex}=(151+151) \div 2=151 {~cm}{/tex}
  • Adding 149 and 152: 140, 145, 148, 149, 150, 151, 151, 152, 152, 153, 155, 160
  • New median {tex}=(151+151) \div 2=151 {~cm}{/tex} (remained same)

In another distribution, the median might change.

questions.question: There were 2 new admissions to Sudhakar’s class just a couple of days after the class average height was found to be 150.2 cm. The heights of the two new joinees are 149 cm and 152 cm.  Which of the following statements about the class average height are correct? Why?

options.option: The information is not sufficient to make a claim about the average.

questions.explanation:
The average of the two new students ({tex}{1 5 0 . 5 ~ c m}{/tex}) is greater than the current class average (150.2 cm). When we add students whose average is higher than the current average, the overall average increases.
Mathematical verification: Let's say there were originally 20 students.

  • Original total height {tex}=150.2 \times 20=3004 {~cm}{/tex}
  • New total height {tex}=3004+149+152=3305 {~cm}{/tex}
  • New average {tex}=3305 \div 22=150.227 \ldots {~cm}>150.2 {~cm}{/tex}

questions.question: There were 2 new admissions to Sudhakar’s class just a couple of days after the class average height was found to be 150.2 cm. Which of the following statements are correct? Why?

options.option: The average height of the class will increase as there are 2 new values.
The heights of everyone in the class has to be measured again to calculate the new average height.
The average height of the class will remain the same.
The heights of the new students have to be measured to find out the new average height.

questions.explanation: We must know the heights of the new students to calculate the new average. Without this information, we cannot determine how the average will change.

questions.question:
A line graph shows the number of visitors to a park for 7 days.
The line rises from Monday to Saturday and drops sharply on Sunday.
Which statement is most appropriate?

options.option: Visitor count increased during the week and fell on Sunday
Visitor count was same on all days

questions.explanation: The upward trend till Saturday and sharp fall on Sunday directly supports this conclusion.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best reason for using a line graph instead of a bar graph to show daily temperature over a year?

options.option: Line graphs show trend and continuous change clearly

questions.explanation: Line graphs highlight continuous change and overall trends over time.

questions.question: If a point on a line graph is plotted at (March, 28), it represents:
questions.explanation: The point shows that the value for March is 28 units.

questions.question:
Two cities A and B are shown on a line graph of monthly rainfall.
City A’s line fluctuates greatly, while City B’s line is nearly flat.
Which inference is most reasonable?

options.option: City A has greater variation in rainfall

questions.explanation: Large ups and downs show greater variation.

questions.question:
A line graph shows temperature from Monday to Friday.
The line is horizontal between Wednesday and Thursday.
This means:
questions.explanation: A horizontal line shows no change.

questions.question: What does a steeper line segment in a line graph generally indicate?
questions.explanation: Steeper lines represent larger change over the same interval.

questions.question: In a line graph, the horizontal axis usually represents:
questions.explanation: Time or order is usually shown on the horizontal axis.

questions.question: A line graph is most suitable to show:
questions.explanation: Line graphs are used to represent change over time.

questions.question:
A value greater than the present median is added to a data set.
Which statement is always correct?

options.option: The median will not be affected in any case

questions.explanation: Adding a value greater than the current median shifts the middle position upward.

questions.question: The median of the data 2, 4, 6, 8 is:
questions.explanation:
There are 4 values.
Median = average of 2nd and 3rd values = (4 + 6) ÷ 2 = 5.

questions.question: The median of the data 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 is:
questions.explanation: After sorting, the middle value is 7.

questions.question: If every value of a data set is multiplied by 3, the new mean becomes:
questions.explanation: Multiplying every value multiplies the mean by the same factor.

questions.question: If every value of a data set is increased by 5, then the new mean is:
questions.explanation: Adding a fixed number to each value increases the mean by the same number.

questions.question:
The mean of 6 numbers is 14. Two numbers 18 and 10 are added.
Which statement is correct?
questions.explanation:
Extra above mean = 18 − 14 = 4
Shortfall below mean = 14 − 10 = 4
They cancel. So the mean remains unchanged.

questions.question: The mean of 8 values is 20. What is the sum of the data?
questions.explanation: Sum = mean × number = 20 × 8 = 160

questions.question:
The mean of a data set is 15. One value equal to 15 is added.
What happens to the mean?
questions.explanation: Adding a value equal to the mean does not change the mean.

questions.question:
The mean of five numbers is 12. A sixth number 18 is added.
The new mean will be:
questions.explanation: Since 18 is greater than the old mean, the new mean increases.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the mean is called a “centre” of the data?

options.option: It is always one of the data values.
Total distance of values on both sides of it is equal.
It lies between the smallest and largest values.

questions.explanation: The mean balances the distances of data values on both sides.

questions.question: The mean of the numbers 4 and 10 is:
questions.explanation: Mean = (4 + 10) ÷ 2 = 14 ÷ 2 = 7

questions.question: Which statement best describes the mean of a data set?

options.option: The difference between largest and smallest values

questions.explanation: The mean represents the balance point of the data on a number line.

questions.question: If a new value of 10 is added to the data set 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, what will the new mean be?
questions.explanation:
Data set: 4, 6, 8, 10, 12

{tex} \text { Sum }=4+6+8+10+12=40{/tex}

New value added = 10
New sum {tex}=40+10=50{/tex}
New number of observations {tex}=6{/tex}
New mean {tex}=50 \div 6=8.33{/tex} (approx)

questions.question: Which of the following data is best represented using a line graph?

options.option: Temperature of a city over a week

questions.explanation: A line graph is best used for data that shows continuous change over time. Temperature over a week changes daily and follows a trend, so it is best represented using a line graph.

questions.question: The average marks of 10 students are 45. One student leaves, and the new average becomes 44. The marks of the student who left is
questions.explanation:
Initial average of 10 students = 45
So, total marks {tex}=10 \times 45=450{/tex}
New average of 9 students {tex}=44{/tex}
So, new total marks {tex}=9 \times 44=396{/tex}
Marks of student who left {tex}=450-396=54{/tex}

questions.question: In a line graph, each plotted point shows
questions.explanation: In a line graph, each plotted point represents a pair of related values-usually one value from the x-axis (like time) and one value from the y-axis (like quantity or measurement). This helps show how two variables are related.

questions.question: A line graph is used to show
questions.explanation: A line graph is used to represent data that changes continuously over time, such as temperature, population growth, or stock prices. It helps in showing trends and patterns clearly.

questions.question: If all values in a data set are equal, then
questions.explanation:
If all values in a data set are equal, then every value is the same number.
So, the mean is that same number, and the median is also that same number.

Therefore, Mean = Median.

questions.question: If the number of observations is even, the median is

options.option: the mean of the two middle terms

questions.explanation: When the number of observations is even, there is no single middle value. So, the median is found by taking the average (mean) of the two middle terms after arranging the data in order.

questions.question: The mean of 10 observations is 25. If each observation is decreased by 5, the new mean is
questions.explanation:
Mean of 10 observations = 25
If each observation is decreased by 5 , the mean also decreases by 5 .
New mean {tex}=25-5=20{/tex}

questions.question: If each observation is multiplied by 3, the mean becomes
questions.explanation: If every observation is multiplied by 3, then their total sum also becomes 3 times. Since mean = sum {tex}\div{/tex} number of observations, the mean also becomes 3 times (triple).

questions.question: The mean of 5 numbers is 20. If one number is 30, the sum of the remaining numbers is
questions.explanation:
Mean of 5 numbers = 20
So, sum of 5 numbers {tex}=20 \times 5=100{/tex}
One number = 30
Sum of remaining 4 numbers {tex}=100-30=70{/tex}

questions.question: The mean of the first 5 natural numbers is
questions.explanation:
The first 5 natural numbers are 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
Mean = (1 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 5) {tex}\div{/tex} 5 = 15 {tex}\div{/tex} 5 = 3.

questions.question: The "Day Length" at a specific location is calculated as:
questions.explanation:
Day length is calculated as the time between sunrise and sunset.
So, Day Length = Sunset time - Sunrise time.

questions.question: If a line on a graph is sloping downwards from left to right, it indicates:
questions.explanation: A line sloping downwards from left to right shows that the value is decreasing as we move forward (usually over time). This indicates a downward trend in the data.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about a line graph?

options.option: It is best for visualising data across time.
It is not suitable for displaying large datasets.
It is used to display categorical data.

questions.explanation: The mean is considered the centre of a data set because it represents the average value of all observations. It balances the data by giving a single central value around which all data points are distributed.

questions.question: Which term is called the ‘centre of the data set’?
questions.explanation: The mean is often called the “centre of the data set” because it represents the average value around which all data points are distributed. It gives a single value that summarizes the entire data set.

questions.question: In the context of data, what is a dot plot used for?

options.option: To display data in a tabular format

questions.explanation: A dot plot is used to display the frequency of data values by placing dots above each value on a number line. Each dot represents one observation, making it easy to see how often each value occurs.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true for the median of a data set?

options.option: The median is the lowest value in the data set.
The median is the highest number in the data set.
The median is the middle value in a sorted data set.
The median is the sum of the values divided by the total number of values.

questions.explanation: The median is the central value of a data set when the data is arranged in ascending or descending order. It divides the data into two equal halves.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about the mean when a value greater than the mean is added?
questions.explanation: If a value greater than the current mean is added to a data set, it pulls the average upward. Therefore, the mean increases.

questions.question: A number is formed as abcabc. It is divisible by:
questions.explanation:
abcabc = 1001(abc)
and
1001 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 13

questions.question: For any three-digit number abc, the sum of abc + bca + cab is always divisible by:
questions.explanation:
{tex} a b c+b c a+c a b=111(a+b+c){/tex}
And, {tex} 111=3 \times 37{/tex}
Therefore, the sum is always divisible by 37.

questions.question: Which of the following is always divisible by 11?

options.option: Sum of a two-digit number and its reverse
Product of a two-digit number and its reverse
Difference of a two-digit number and its reverse

questions.question:
Let a two-digit number be 10a + b.
The difference between the number and its reverse is:
If three digits are p < q < r, the arrangement that gives the largest product is:
questions.explanation: Largest digit as multiplier and remaining digits in decreasing order.

questions.question: Using digits 3, 5 and 9, the largest product is:
questions.explanation:
Find each product:
{tex}35 \times 9=315{/tex}
{tex}53 \times 9=477{/tex}
{tex}59 \times 3=177{/tex}
{tex}95 \times 3=285{/tex}

The largest product is:

{tex} 53 \times 9=477{/tex}

questions.question: Using digits 1, 3 and 7 once each, the largest possible product is:
questions.explanation: Largest digit as multiplier; others in descending order → 31 × 7.

questions.question: If the top-left number of a 2 × 2 calendar grid is 11, what is the bottom-right number?
questions.explanation: Bottom-right = a + 8 = 11 + 8 = 19

questions.question:
The sum of a 2 × 2 calendar block is 52.
Find the top-left number.
questions.explanation: {tex}4 a+16=52 \Rightarrow 4 a=36 \Rightarrow a=9{/tex}

questions.question: In a calendar, the top-left number of a {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} block is a. What is the sum of the block?
questions.explanation:
Numbers are {tex}a, a+1, a+7, a+8{/tex}.
Sum {tex}=4 a+16{/tex}.

questions.question: For a pyramid with bottom row a, b, c, d, what is the top expression?
questions.explanation: Coefficients follow 1, 3, 3, 1.

questions.question:
Bottom row of a pyramid is: 4, 1, 6
What is the top number?
questions.explanation:
{tex} 4+2(1)+6=12 \text { (Check pyramid) } {/tex}
Middle row: 5 and 7
Top = 5 + 7 = 12

questions.question: For a 3-row pyramid with bottom entries a, b, c, the top number is:
questions.explanation: Standard result: {tex} (a+b)+(b+c)=a+2 b+c {/tex}

questions.question:
In a number pyramid, the bottom row is: 3, 5, 7
What is the number at the top?
questions.explanation:
{tex} \text {Top }=a+2 b+c {/tex}
3 + 2(5) + 7 = 20 (Wait) ⇒ Check carefully
Actual pyramid:
Middle row: {tex}3+5=8,5+7=12{/tex}
{tex} \text {Top }=8+12=20 {/tex}

questions.question: A student wants the final answer of a trick to be 10. Which of the following expressions is suitable?
questions.explanation: {tex}x+10-x=10{/tex}

questions.question:
A trick gives the final expression
5x - 2x + 1 - 3x
What is the final result?
A trick gives the final result as 6 for every number chosen. Which expression can represent the final step?
questions.explanation: {tex}x+6-x=6{/tex}

questions.question: Which of the following expressions will always give the same value for any starting number {tex}x{/tex}?
questions.explanation: {tex}5 x-5 x+4=4{/tex}

questions.question:
A student performs these operations on a number x:
Multiply by 4, add 12, then divide by 4, then subtract the original number.
What is the final answer?
questions.explanation:
{tex} 4 x+12 \rightarrow \frac{4 x+12}{4}=x+3 {/tex}
{tex} (x+3)-x=3 {/tex}

questions.question:
A number trick starts with a number {tex}x{/tex}. After some steps, the final expression becomes
3x + 7 - 3x
What will be the final result?
For what value of x , a number of the form {tex}({x} \times 100+6 \times 10+8){/tex} is divisible by 3?
questions.explanation:
Number is:

{tex} x \times 100+6 \times 10+8={/tex}{tex}100 x+60+8=100 x+68{/tex}

For divisibility by 3, sum of digits must be divisible by 3.
Digits are: {tex}x, 6,8{/tex}
Sum:

{tex} x+6+8=x+14{/tex}

Now check options:

  • {tex}x=7 \Rightarrow 7+14=21{/tex} (divisible by 3)
  • others do not give a multiple of 3

So, required value is 7.

questions.question: Rohan uses a trick to find a friend's birthday. He tells his friend to multiply the month number M by 5, add 7, multiply by 4, add 13, and finally multiply the result by 5. Which algebraic expression represents this trick?
questions.explanation:
Let the month number be {tex}M{/tex}
Step by step:

  1. Multiply by {tex}5 \rightarrow 5 M{/tex}
  2. Add {tex}7 \rightarrow 5 M+7{/tex}
  3. Multiply by {tex}4 \rightarrow(5 M+7) \times 4{/tex}
  4. Add {tex}13 \rightarrow(5 M+7) \times 4+13{/tex}
  5. Multiply the whole result by {tex}5 \rightarrow{/tex}{tex} \{(5 M+7) \times 4+13\} \times 5{/tex}

questions.question: Priya thinks of a number x. She doubles it, adds 10, divides the result by 2, and finally subtracts the original number x. What is her final answer?
questions.explanation:
Let the number be {tex}x{/tex}

  1. Double: {tex}2 x{/tex}
  2. Add {tex}10: 2 x+10{/tex}
  3. Divide by 2: {tex}\frac{2 x+10}{2}=x+5{/tex}
  4. Subtract original number {tex}x{/tex}: {tex} (x+5)-x=5{/tex}

So the final answer is 5, independent of {tex}x{/tex}.

questions.question: 37037 is always divisible by
questions.explanation:
Check 37037:

{tex} 37037 \div 17=2179{/tex}

So it divides exactly.

questions.question: The number 1001 is not divisible by
questions.explanation:
Check divisibility of 1001:

{tex}1001 \div 1=1001{/tex}
{tex}1001 \div 11=91{/tex} {tex}\text { (so divisible by } 11 \text { ) }{/tex}
{tex}1001 \div 13=77{/tex}
{tex}1001 \div 17=58.88 {/tex} (not exact)

So, 1001 is not divisible by 17.

questions.question: Make the largest product possible using digits 4, 7, and 3.
questions.explanation:
We arrange digits 4, 7, 3 to form two numbers for maximum product.
Best arrangement:

{tex} 7 \times 43=301{/tex}

Other arrangements:

  • {tex}7 \times 34=238{/tex}
  • {tex}4 \times 73=292{/tex}
  • {tex}3 \times 74=222{/tex}

So the largest product is:

{tex} 301{/tex}

questions.question: Make the largest product possible using digits 2, 3, and 5.
questions.explanation:
We have digits 2, 3, 5 and we must form numbers to get the maximum product.
Try best arrangement:

  • {tex}5 \times 32=160{/tex}

Other possibilities give smaller products:

  • {tex}3 \times 52=156{/tex}
  • {tex}2 \times 53=106{/tex}

So the largest product is:

{tex} 160{/tex}

questions.question: Find the sum of a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 grid, if the top left number of the grid is 11.
questions.explanation:
In this grid pattern, numbers are arranged as:

{tex}\begin{array}{ll} 11 & 12 \\ 17 & 20 \end{array}{/tex}

Now add all numbers:

{tex} 11+12+17+20=60{/tex}
So, the sum of the {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} grid is 60.

questions.question:
Think of a date {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}  Add 10 to the month {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} multiply by 5 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} subtract 40 from it {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} multiply by 4 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} again subtract it by 40 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} multiply it by 5.
What is the constant obtained in the above trick?
questions.explanation:
Let the month be {tex}m{/tex}

  1. {tex}m+10{/tex}
  2. {tex}\times 5=5 m+50{/tex}
  3. {tex}-40=5 m+10{/tex}
  4. {tex}\times 4=20 m+40{/tex}
  5. {tex}-40=20 m{/tex}
  6. {tex}\times 5=100 {~m}{/tex}

Final result {tex}=100 {~m}{/tex}

Since the answer depends on the month {tex}m{/tex}, it is not a constant.

questions.question: Think of a number {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} multiply it by {tex}2 \rightarrow{/tex} add {tex}2 \rightarrow{/tex} divide it by {tex}2 \rightarrow{/tex} add {tex}2 \rightarrow{/tex} multiply it by {tex}3 \rightarrow{/tex} subtract the number by a third of the number you thought. Tick the number you find at the end.
questions.explanation:
Let the number be {tex}x{/tex}

  1. {tex}x \times 2=2 x{/tex}
  2. {tex}2 x+2{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{2 x+2}{2}=x+1{/tex}
  4. {tex}x+1+2=x+3{/tex}
  5. {tex}(x+3) \times 3=3 x+9{/tex}

Now subtract the original number and one-third of it (as intended in the trick):

{tex} 3 x+9-x-\frac{x}{3}=3 x+9-\frac{4 x}{3}=9{/tex}

So the final answer is 9.

questions.question:
